stem: A group life insurance contract is a legal agreement between the insurer and
options: the employer or the scheme trustees.
explanation: The group life insurance contract is between the insurer and the policyholder, which is either the employer or the scheme trustees. Individual members are not parties to the contract.

stem: Under the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999, a third party may enforce a term of a contract if
options: the contract expressly provides for it or the term purports to confer a benefit on the third party.
the third party is a family member of one of the contracting parties.
the third party has paid a consideration.
the contract has been in force for at least 12 months.
explanation: The Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999 allows a third party to enforce a contractual term if the contract expressly provides for this, or if the term purports to confer a benefit on the third party.

stem: In relation to group risk insurance, the doctrine of 'utmost good faith' requires
options: both parties to disclose all material facts.
only the insurer to disclose all material facts.
only the policyholder to disclose all material facts.
neither party to disclose material facts after the policy is issued.
explanation: The duty of utmost good faith (uberrimae fidei) requires both the insurer and the policyholder to disclose all material facts relevant to the insurance contract.

stem: Under the Equality Act 2010, an employer providing group risk benefits must not discriminate on the basis of
options: age, disability, gender reassignment and other protected characteristics.
explanation: The Equality Act 2010 protects against discrimination on the basis of nine protected characteristics including age, disability, gender reassignment, marriage and civil partnership, pregnancy and maternity, race, religion or belief, sex, and sexual orientation.

stem: Under the Equality Act 2010, an insurer may use age as a factor in pricing group risk insurance provided that
options: the Financial Conduct Authority gives specific approval.
it can be justified by relevant and reliable actuarial or statistical data.
it is only applied to members over age 50.
explanation: The Equality Act 2010 permits the use of age in insurance pricing provided it can be justified by reference to relevant and reliable actuarial or statistical data.

stem: The lump sum and death benefit allowance (LSDBA) replaced which previous limit on pension benefits?
explanation: The lump sum and death benefit allowance replaced the lifetime allowance as the limit on tax-free lump sum death benefits from registered pension schemes.

stem: What is the current level of the lump sum and death benefit allowance?
explanation: The lump sum and death benefit allowance is set at £1,073,100, which is the same level as the former lifetime allowance.

stem: Under current pension legislation, how are death benefits in excess of the lump sum and death benefit allowance taxed?
options: They are taxed at the basic rate of Income Tax.
They are taxed at the recipient's marginal rate of Income Tax.
They are subject to a flat 55% charge.
explanation: Lump sum death benefits in excess of the LSDBA are taxed at the recipient's marginal rate of Income Tax.

stem: Under UK data protection legislation, the lawful basis most commonly used by insurers to process personal data for underwriting and claims purposes is
explanation: Insurers most commonly rely on 'legitimate interests' as the lawful basis for processing personal data for underwriting and claims purposes under UK GDPR.

stem: Under the Employment Rights Act 1996, what is the minimum qualifying period of continuous service before an employee gains protection against unfair dismissal?
explanation: An employee must have at least two years of continuous service with the same employer before gaining protection against ordinary unfair dismissal under the Employment Rights Act 1996.

stem: The Consumer Insurance (Disclosure and Representations) Act 2012 requires a consumer applying for insurance to
options: disclose all material facts whether or not asked about them.
take reasonable care not to make a misrepresentation.
warrant the accuracy of all statements made.
explanation: Under the Consumer Insurance (Disclosure and Representations) Act 2012, the duty on the consumer is to take reasonable care not to make a misrepresentation when answering questions from the insurer.

stem: For an employer-sponsored group life scheme to obtain tax advantages, it must normally be established under
options: a master trust or standalone trust deed.
explanation: A group life scheme that provides tax-advantaged benefits is normally established under a trust arrangement, either a master trust or standalone trust deed, allowing the trustees to hold and distribute benefits.

stem: A registered pension scheme that provides death-in-service benefits must comply with rules set by
explanation: Registered pension schemes, including those providing death-in-service benefits, must comply with the rules and conditions set by HM Revenue & Customs to maintain their tax-advantaged status.

stem: Under data protection legislation, an individual has the right to request access to their personal data held by an insurer. This is known as a
explanation: A subject access request (SAR) is the mechanism by which an individual can request access to the personal data held about them by an organisation, including an insurer.

stem: The Insurance Act 2015 introduced what standard of disclosure for non-consumer insurance contracts?
options: A duty of fair presentation of risk.
A duty of utmost good faith.
A duty to volunteer all known risks.
explanation: The Insurance Act 2015 introduced the duty of fair presentation of risk for non-consumer (business) insurance contracts, requiring the insured to disclose material circumstances in a reasonably clear and accessible manner.

stem: Under the Equality Act 2010, ceasing group life cover when a member reaches a specific normal retirement age is
options: lawful if it can be objectively justified.
only lawful if the member agrees in writing.
only permitted for members over age 75.
explanation: Ceasing group life cover at normal retirement age can constitute age discrimination under the Equality Act 2010, but it may be lawful if the employer can show it is a proportionate means of achieving a legitimate aim.

stem: The annual allowance for pension contributions can be reduced for high earners through the tapered annual allowance. The minimum tapered annual allowance is currently
explanation: For very high earners, the tapered annual allowance reduces the standard annual allowance down to a minimum of £10,000.

stem: The trustees of a discretionary trust for a group death-in-service scheme are expected to consider a member's expression of wish form but are
options: only bound by it if it is less than two years old.
only bound by it if it has been witnessed.
explanation: An expression of wish form guides the trustees in distributing a death benefit, but the trustees of a discretionary trust are not legally bound by it. They retain discretion to determine the most appropriate distribution.

stem: Limited Price Indexation (LPI) for an occupational pension scheme established before 6 April 2005 requires pension increases at a rate capped at
explanation: For occupational pension schemes established before 6 April 2005, Limited Price Indexation requires pension increases capped at 5% or the Consumer Price Index if less.

stem: Under group risk insurance, the role of the Financial Ombudsman Service is to
options: investigate and resolve disputes between consumers and financial firms.
set premium rates for group insurance contracts.
explanation: The Financial Ombudsman Service provides an independent dispute resolution service for consumers who have complaints against financial firms, including insurers.
